name: Plains Cree

formality.description: Plains Cree does not have a European-style T-V distinction. Formality is expressed through the use of respectful particles and verb forms. The language has a complex polysynthetic morphology — a single word can encode what requires an entire sentence in English. SRO (Standard Roman Orthography) is the working script; Syllabics (ᓀᐦᐃᔭᐍᐏᐣ) is the display script.

standard.label: Standard (SRO)
standard.description: Standard Plains Cree in SRO orthography. Clear, accessible language appropriate for educational and community software.
standard.prompt: Standard Plains Cree (nêhiyawêwin) in SRO (Standard Roman Orthography). Use established vocabulary from published Cree dictionaries. When no standard term exists for a technical concept, keep the English term rather than inventing neologisms. Syllabics conversion is handled as a post-processing step.

encyclopedic.history: Plains Cree is the most widely spoken Algonquian language in Canada. Historically, it functioned as a regional trade lingua franca across the Canadian plains.

linguisticChallenges.polysynthesis: Cree is highly polysynthetic. A single verb can incorporate subject/object pronouns, instrumentals, locations, and actions. Translate phrases conceptually rather than word-for-word.
linguisticChallenges.animacy: Verb conjugation changes completely based on whether the subject/object nouns are animate or inanimate. Ensure grammatical agreement.
linguisticChallenges.neologisms: Avoid literal translations of modern software concepts. Maintain English loanwords for modern UI elements when no community-accepted Cree term exists.

notes: Low-resource language under active development. Translation quality requires llm-coached method with Cree-specific coaching data. See OCAP principles documentation for community engagement guidelines.
